data.title: Anfield Energy Is A High-Risk, High-Reward Play For Uranium
data.description:
Anfield Energy (AEC) has been one of the top-performing stocks of the entire stock market in the last 12 months. The stock has surged 93%, and thus it has outperformed the S&P 500, which has gained 17%, by an impressive margin. The outstanding performance of Anfield Energy has resulted from the bright outlook of nuclear power and the ideal positioning of the company to benefit from the expected boom of nuclear power. The big question is whether the stock remains attractive after such a steep rally.

Business overview

Anfield Energy is engaged in the exploration, development, and production of mineral properties in the U.S. It is primarily focused on uranium, vanadium, and gold properties. Uranium is the key input of nuclear reactors, while vanadium is a vital metal in many industries, such as construction, aerospace, and automotive.

Nuclear power was suffering from negative public sentiment for decades due to two catastrophic accidents, namely the accident at Chernobyl, Ukraine, in 1986 and the accident in Fukushima, Japan, in 2011. Due to these two accidents, the global energy produced by nuclear plants has remained essentially flat over the last two decades.

Source: World Nuclear Association

However, the global energy landscape is currently going through a major transition. The demand for energy is likely to grow significantly in the upcoming years thanks to the secular boom of AI data centers. As nearly all countries are doing their best to curtail the consumption of fossil fuels, the incremental demand should be covered with clean energy sources. Solar and wind power plants are clean sources of energy, but they are not 100% reliable, as they cannot generate energy on a continuous basis due to the volatile nature of weather. Therefore, nuclear power is widely viewed as the ideal solution, as it is both clean and continuous.

Uranium is the primary input of nuclear reactors, and hence the demand for uranium is likely to greatly increase in the upcoming years. In addition, supply is limited, as there has been very low investment in uranium exploration over the last two decades due to the aforementioned negative public sentiment over nuclear power. Therefore, the outlook for future uranium prices appears favorable.

Anfield Energy is ideally positioned to benefit from the expected boom in the global demand for uranium.

Source: Investor Presentation

One of its primary assets, the Shootaring Canyon Mill in Utah, is one of only three licensed uranium mills in the U.S. This asset is highly promising, as it is located in one of the most prolific production areas in the U.S. The company applied for a production restart to Utah’s Department of Environmental Quality and received a positive response in 2024. Whenever Anfield Energy resumes production at this site, it intends to increase the throughput capacity of the mill from 750 to 1000 tons per day.

Source: Investor Presentation

It is worth noting that the Shootaring Canyon Mill was producing uranium at some point in the early 80s, but it stopped production in 1982 due to weak uranium prices. It has remained idle for more than four decades, but it is about to return to operation thanks to the favorable outlook of the uranium business. Management expects the project to return to operation in the second half of this year.

It is also important to note that the U.S. government approved the Velvet-Wood project of Anfield Energy via a fast-track 14-day review process last year. This project was the first uranium project to be approved via an emergency process, thus revealing the will of the U.S. government to do its best to enhance the domestic energy supplies. The ease of approval certainly bodes well for the growth prospects of Anfield Energy, as it is indicative of a very friendly political environment.

Notably, Uranium Energy (UEC), a much larger uranium producer with a market capitalization of $6.6 billion, doubled its stake in Anfield Energy last year and thus it now has a 32.4% stake in the company. This is undoubtedly a strong vote of confidence of Uranium Energy in the growth prospects of Anfield Energy.

Valuation

Anfield Energy has yet to generate revenues and earnings. As a result, the stock cannot be evaluated based on the well-known conventional metrics, such as price-to-earnings and price-to-sales ratios.

On the other hand, one can have an idea of the upside potential of the stock based on a rough comparison to Uranium Energy. According to the latest presentation of Anfield Energy, Uranium Energy has total uranium resources that are 7.5 times greater than those of Anfield Energy (365 vs. 49 Mlbs). It also has a market capitalization that is 65 times greater than that of Anfield Energy ($6.6 billion vs. $0.101 billion).

On the one hand, Uranium Energy is a more established and much less speculative company, and hence it warrants a high premium over Anfield Energy. On the other hand, it is evident that Anfield Energy has great upside potential if the favorable business landscape related to nuclear power remains in place.

Risks

Anfield Energy is different from other uranium producers, such as Uranium Energy and Cameco (CCJ), as the former is not generating revenues. It has posted losses in 9 of the last 10 years, and thus it has diluted its shareholders to a great extent to fund its obligations. To be sure, its share count has more than quadrupled over the last five years. If Anfield Energy executes its restart plan without any setbacks, it is likely to highly reward its shareholders. On the other hand, if its project is greatly delayed, it will have to issue many new shares, and thus its stock price is likely to come under pressure.

In the six months leading to September 2025 (this is the most recent report of the company), Anfield Energy reported a decrease of $4.5 million in its cash position. In other words, its cash burn rate is approximately $9.0 million per year. As the last cash position reported was $5.2 million, it was evident that the company would need to issue new shares. Indeed, Anfield Energy agreed to issue 50,000 new shares and 500,000 common share purchase warrants in January. Going forward, the company may need to dilute its shareholders further. Only the investors who are comfortable with this risk should consider buying the stock.

Final thoughts

Anfield Energy is a speculative stock, as it has yet to generate revenues. However, thanks to the highly promising prospects of nuclear energy amid a boom in energy demand from AI data centers, the stock appears attractive, particularly given the favorable stance of the U.S. government towards energy projects. Moreover, the aforementioned project at Shootaring Canyon Mill is expected to come online in just a few months, and hence there is minimal execution risk, in contrast to the execution risk of long-term exploration projects. Nevertheless, as the company has not generated any revenues or earnings yet, it carries an elevated amount of risk, and hence investors should allocate a limited portion of their capital to this stock.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: AIPI: From Capped Upside To Rangebound Income Opportunity
data.description:
In May last year, I had issued a Hold rating for the REX AI Equity Premium Income ETF (AIPI). At that time, and for a few months following that rating, everything tagged AI was being lapped up by the market. That environment has changed. The AI trade has cooled, macro pressures and stagflation risks aside. Importantly, the core objective for income seekers is to be able to generate optimal income, whether through an option writing route or through capital appreciation of the underlying.

Last year AIPI was not posing much upside from the AI rally because of the capped nature of the covered call writing. Income was still supportive because some upside capture was still at play, although despite the option income, the aggressive yields meant NAV erosion possibilities, which is bad news for future payout size. You can see the effect of smaller payouts over time in the dividend payout chart below. This is despite any meaningful change in yield levels. That effect only amplifies if the underlying portfolio stagnates.

Theoretically, the strategy does not need a sharp rally in the underlying. What is actually and optimally needed is a supportive portfolio as a minimum, which then opens up option income in rangebound markets. The total returns can, in such a regime, even outperform the underlying portfolio itself. That regime is here today — after a reset in the AI trade, we could see exactly the perfect markets for AIPI. One where the risks of a further downside is more limited than it was in May last year, and the odds of a sharp rally also doesn't look imminent. A rangebound market is where option income works best. In short, compared to my old thesis, where the upside opportunity loss was far higher than the drawdown mitigation, we have now moved into a more symmetric market for option writing. That moves AIPI to Buy territory.

Performance Since May

The below chart shows the total return trajectory for AIPI, with AIQ and QQQ as anchors to help contextualize. Neither of the anchors have the same exact underlying portfolio as AIPI, but AIQ should show an approximate path for total return investors invested in the AI theme (AIPI has a much smaller high conviction portfolio of ~25 stocks compared to AIQ's broader ~90 stock exposure), and QQQ shows an even broader AI path without AIPI's high conviction positioning.

The total returns chart shows how AIPI expectedly was unable to capture the AI trade's upside in a strong bull market till October 2025, something even a broader AI portfolio like AIQ could. AIPI's total return trajectory (implies payouts reinvested and option income embedded on top of portfolio gains) traced a very broad exposure to AI like QQQ in the bull run. That often means bad news for holding through the cycle - if one of the sharpest AI rallies only means tracking a broader AI portfolio (like QQQ), then in even slightly cooling markets, the downside will be far higher. This is also in line with what we see, AIPI's upside is QQQ but the downside is probably as high as AIQ, and this despite the option income.

That also means the expected NAV erosion starts showing up aggressively in flat to negative regimes for the AI trade. While the NAV erosion threat was broadly contained till October, we have now seen 20-30% NAV erosion. The performance of AIPI since May overall upholds the concerns I had around the time of the earlier thesis. For a strategy that at best retains NAV in one of the best times for its underlying portfolio; NAV erosion should be expected to be the norm in most market conditions.

That is unlikely to be fixed ahead unless yields are moderated or reinvested. But three things matter in addition to NAV erosion — one, the quality of income, two, the degree of NAV erosion and three, the asymmetry of upside and downside moves. On these parameters, AIPI seems to have moved into a better regime today. And all those themes are inter-related too. A cooler regime for AI valuations imply we are no longer staring at capturing say 2% upside when underlying holdings are rallying by 10%, yet staring at all the risks of cooling ahead (like we were in May to October 2025), with minimal cushion from option income in drawdowns. That makes it a more symmetrical setup for AIPI now. A flat rangebound outlook also means slower upsides are captured, option premiums add to the total returns and overall, the quality of income is option dominated (rather than a return of capital which is structurally not allowed to grow well either due to the covered call upside capping setup). Since the underlying is flat, NAV erodes by the difference of payouts versus option income. This is an improvement from the most recent drawdown regime where NAV erodes due to corrections in the underlying as well.

Why The Rangebound View

A look at AIPI's portfolio explains my rangebound view on the portfolio. Around 70% of the portfolio is in enablers and adopters, with high beta and uncertainties at the moment. The enablers exposure is primarily in semis and AI infrastructure, where late cycle concerns are emerging. The trough in hyperscaler CapEx could indeed turn out to be higher than what markets fear at this point and AI could be the multi-decade secular tailwind it was positioned as, at the start of the cycle. Even if cyclicality returns in the semis space, we could see that later than the immediate next year too. But the odds of a multiple expansion definitely looks less possible at the moment. We do not have to move too far beyond the NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) stagnation over the past few months to verify this rangebound outlook in the semis space. I have written about how the tech heavyweights will likely remain rangebound unless earnings upgrades return, in a separate article on the Schwab US Large-Cap Growth ETF (SCHG).

The next biggest exposure is in an even more uncertain bucket of adopters, dominated primarily by SaaS companies. This is where markets are gauging the effects of a steady state AI dynamics, commoditization risks and pricing pressures thereafter. While the bulk of corrections have already happened here, there is no urgency to rerate the pack (except a few pockets), unless certainty arrives. And that uncertainty will linger for a few quarters at least as the tech world evolves through AI's capabilities and positioning.

The hyperscaler pack is probably the most resilient group in AIPI's portfolio, but it is underweight compared to semis and SaaS. These companies are also going through their own CapEx drag pressures, but monetization uncertainties are not as binary as in the case of SaaS or cyclical as in the case of semis. The bigger question for this layer is the return on capex and the economics of the AI monetization phase. Some of the companies in this pack, like Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) or Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) have corrected quite a bit too acknowledging the high spend regime. So I expect this layer to be relatively stable, but we are certainly not looking at immediate upsides, at least at a 2025-like pace.

How Well is AIPI Set Up?

AIPI writes calls on individual holdings rather than at an index level. I downloaded the full holdings as of April 2nd 2026 and saw covered calls written for almost the full holding notional value for the top 10 holdings. There are some mechanisms like a laddered call setup which means some upside capture is not ruled out (with some strikes far OTM), but this is an aggressive option writing setup as such.

That aggressive setup with a bit of upside room for underlying stocks is the perfect recipe for the current rangebound outlook, as we have discussed before. It ensures the portfolio generates flat to marginally positive returns over the next few quarters and the option layer generates decent income, especially if conditions remain as volatile as we have seen over the past month.

A Buy Call for the Next Few Quarters

A Buy for an income plan does not necessarily mean buying the underlying portfolio. So the AI tag does not help AIPI's thesis as much as it looks like. Income strategies should look at a moderately bullish thesis in the underlying, in the long term which AI comes with. That thesis is strengthened after massive corrections in the SaaS space and some cooling in semis and hyperscaler stocks. And beyond a point, such strategies do not need a sharp bull run versus a moderate one - beyond a threshold, the upside does not matter much (especially if it is within a short period of time). To that end, I was wary of the disproportionate downside risks in May last year, without commensurate benefits of the sharp upside getting captured. That thesis has now turned to a Buy. I expect the underlying portfolio to now be in an ideal position to keep the overall upside under check. Downside risks are not as high as earlier. And the option income should be able to add value than just cap upsides.

data.title: Texas Pacific Land Corporation: Still Too Expensive
data.description:
The last article on Texas Pacific Land Corporation (TPL) mentioned that the stock was extremely expensive for the growth characteristics of the company. Since then, the Iran situation that includes effectively closing the Strait of Hormuz has basically sent everything in the industry much higher. However, such issues rarely last long. Because the price-earnings ratio remains very high, this particular idea has a lot more downside potential when things settle down than do many of my other choices.

Stock Price

The last article showed the Quant system ranking this as a Strong Sell. But the Iranian situation changed that ranking because of the stock price action. The Quant system now has the stock as a Hold.

It is very easy to see the effect that the Iranian situation has had on the stock price from the chart above. That was definitely an unknown at the time the last article was written.

However, this is not an industry that is known for price-earnings ratios of 52 for really much of any reason. This stock has long been an exception to much of the industry. As such, it is really just a question as to when the stock price will value the business in the same fashion as much of the rest of the industry.

Pricing like this can persist for a while. The Iranian situation is far from settled. Therefore, commodity prices can go a lot higher before they inevitably decline. The timing of anything in the future is very much unsettled right now in the eyes of the market.

The Business

The company has two main revenue streams currently with some future possibilities.

The land it has it farms out on a royalty basis to form one main revenue stream. This business is basically an upstream business without the major risks of upstream like dry holes or the expenses of drilling and completion. The company largely makes its money as a passive entity as it does not control development of the resources on its leases. Keep in mind that this is a simple overview.

A little later came the water business. That is more a midstream business of providing water services to companies developing its resources on an as needed basis.

Now there is talk of some other ventures like possibly some data centers in the future. But right now, the two main businesses were already described.

Earnings

For the latest fiscal year, earnings came in at roughly $7 per share. For much of the industry, earnings like that would justify a stock price at best of possibly $70.

This is a cyclical industry. Therefore, the market will generally value a company based upon its average profitability. Now thanks to unconventional upstream and the water business, this is a cyclical growth story. But this industry is not the tech industry. So large price-earnings ratios as is seen here are at best few and far between. Additionally, they rarely last.

This is obviously a very profitable business as shown by the large percentage that Net Income would be of Revenue. But the growth rate for earnings (as shown above) is in the single digits.

Earnings growth can be influenced by declining commodity prices as was likely the case in fiscal year 2024. Much of the valuation process depends upon whether the market focuses on production per share growth or earnings per share growth. But a big consideration is that this seems to be a momentum-based market that may focus on positive comparisons (period) with no excuses. A cyclical downturn may not be tolerated by the market.

What has changed since the last article is the ascendency of commodity prices. Now that could make for roughly a doubling of earnings per share (at least while it lasts). But it also brings about a price-earnings ratio contraction because the market prices these (lower or less valuable) for above average earnings during periods of high commodity prices (because those great periods do not last).

I am thinking that the Iranian situation will mark a cyclical top for the industry even if that cyclical top does not last long. But much will depend upon the supply of various commodities after the situation ends (and how fast damages get repaired as well).

Complicating this is the fact that Venezuela is now in a position to rebuild its production as well. As usual, timing would be uncertain.

But it does seem like the currently high prices will be replaced at some point in the future by a period of abundance and probably low commodity prices.

Realize that this is a very low visibility industry where things can change very fast with little warning.

Balance Sheet

If you go to the SEC filings on the company website (page 4) and click on the 10-K, it is easy to see that the company has no long-term debt. This is typical for companies with a royalty business. But that means that the company has both the cash flow and the financial flexibility to pursue new businesses with its land or to return the money to shareholders through share buybacks or dividends.

Risk

Any upstream business, including a royalty business is dependent upon the level of commodity prices. For this reason, it is best to consider any company with a royalty business as a variable distribution entity.

To help defend the dividend there is a stock repurchase program that can be eliminated first in the event of a severe and sustained downturn that would necessitate such actions.

Similarly, the level of commodity prices also influences the general upstream activity level and hence the per share production growth. An unforeseen severe and sustained commodity price decline could also eliminate the business growth for a while as well.

The opposite is true as well. A sustained upturn in commodity prices would enhance (at least temporarily) growth prospects.

The water business is a bit steadier because as production ages, the wells often produce more water. This counter cyclical nature would partially offset the lower need for water for fracking during a cyclical downturn.

Summary

The current price-earnings ratio is not justified by the earnings growth rate. There may be a one-time large earnings boost as commodity prices rise to accommodate the situation that arises as a result of the Iranian crisis. But once the Iranian crisis is settled there is likely to be a cyclical downturn at least on the horizon.

For the time being, the growth rate does not appear to support a price-earnings ratio of 53. As commodity prices rise, cyclical stocks often have lower price-earnings ratios because the market knows that high commodity prices do not last in a free market. The cure for high prices is high prices in a free market.

While I personally believe that a price-earnings ratio of 10 is plenty for the financial characteristics shown in the article. The final number when we get there could well depend upon inflation, interest rates, and whether or not the industry is a market favorite.

Let us assume that commodity prices are high enough to justify an earnings rate of $15 per share annually while the Iranian situation lasts. That would price the stock around $150. That is far below the current price. It will not get any better when the Iranian situation is over unless we get another surprise.

But I have no way of justifying a price-earnings ratio of even 20 under the most optimistic scenarios.

Therefore, for me, this remains a Strong Sell because I believe that I can find far cheaper alternatives that have the same financial characteristics or better.

That does not mean that the current situation will not see the price go higher. But it does mean that for me, the current valuation is not sustainable long-term. Therefore, it is very possible that a trading profit opportunity exists but not a buy-and-hold opportunity.

data.title: Workday: SaaSpocalypse Selloff Creates A Golden Opportunity
data.description:
With fears of an AI-induced software apocalypse hitting markets, Workday, Inc. (WDAY) clearly hasn't been spared in the carnage. As shown in the chart below, the stock has seen losses of over 40% in the past six months and the bulk of those declines have occurred this year. Interestingly, despite being a tech focused analyst, I haven't initiated coverage on this SaaS giant yet. Given the current state of things, I believe that now is the right time to take a look at their fundamentals and give readers my analysis.

Below, little signs of a SaaSpocalypse are found. Growth actually accelerated in their Q4 and margins expanded. Moving forward, guidance for FY2027 was decent but slower margin expansion may be a near term risk as the company continues to invest in AI. For long term investors this may actually be a good sign. With the forward P/E now at clearly depressed levels, I have decided to initiate Workday at a buy rating as the end of the line for the company is really nowhere in sight.

AI Integration

While the market is fretting about AI disruption for Workday, in reality the company has integrated AI deep within their enterprise solutions. In just a second, we'll see how successful the company has been in monetizing this but at least it is clear from the above infographic that they haven't been slow to adapt to a changing landscape. As you can see, they have a strong ecosystem of agents including Workday Agents and so it seems that the company has been quick to react to the boom in agentic AI.

Growth Accelerated

Workday reported their FY2026 Q4 earnings in late February. Let's start with the top line numbers. The company generated total revenues of $2.532 billion, up 14.5% YoY. This is an acceleration of growth from the previous quarter's 12.6% increase and so it actually seems that Workday's business trajectory is improving. For their subscription revenues specifically, there was also growth acceleration to 15.7%.

In the year ago quarter, company-wide growth was at 15% and so really the company has shown great resilience. While this shows that AI hasn't created strong tailwinds, it also shows that AI hasn't caused apocalyptic results like many had feared. Workday also managed to beat top line expectations by $8.81 million and so the fourth quarter was one of outperformance.

Lastly, it should be mentioned here that Workday does have quite a respectable gross revenue retention. Currently, this metric is at 97% and so that shows most clients decide to continue using their services. While an even higher level in retention is always desired, I would say that the current position is enough to suggest that Workday is offering a decent value proposition to customers and that is making them stay for the most part.

Margin Expands

Another sign that the apocalypse hasn't materialized so far is the fact that margin actually expanded by a healthy amount in Q4. Workday reported a non-GAAP operating margin of 30.6%, up 413 basis points from the prior year period's 26.4%. While this obviously shows strong cost and/or expense control, it also shows that the company's pricing power hasn't been eroded significantly. If Workday's solutions were truly getting disrupted by AI, their pricing power would likely evaporate and so the fact that margin has held up quite well suggests that fears are overblown.

With operating margin being healthy, the bottom line was also robust for Q4. As shown above, they reported an adjusted EPS of $2.47, up 28.65% YoY. This is a nice acceleration from the previous quarter and also represents a beat on the bottom line. We'll get into their guidance next, but consensus expectations for EPS do seem a little concerning as we head into their FY2027.

Guidance Looks Respectable

Firstly, Workday's subscription revenue guidance for Q1 was below expectations and so there may be some disappointment there. However, let's take a look at their full year estimates to see where things are going on a longer timeframe.

For total revenue growth, they are projecting between 11% and 12% for FY2027. That would be a notable slowdown from FY2026's 13.1% increase. Why that is definitely not favorable, I would say that this deceleration is far from what would be classified as apocalyptic. For subscription revenues, a moderate slowdown is also expected from 14.5% to the range of 12% to 13% and so again, nothing catastrophic.

As for margin, Workday sees non-GAAP operating margin expansion of 44 basis points. While this isn't nearly as impressive as FY2026's 368 basis point improvement, there is likely to be expansion nonetheless. Therefore, core profitability of the company is expected to stay resilient. As mentioned earlier, major business disruption rarely results in margin expansion and so there are further signals that Workday is in fact holding its ground rather nicely.

On their earnings call, CFO Zane Rowe stated that this outlook takes into account "an accelerated pace of AI investment across both product and go-to-market." He went on to say that "we remain focused on both GAAP and non-GAAP margin expansion, albeit at a slower pace in the near term than what we previously communicated." Analysts also highlighted short-term margin risk post earnings. In my view, while this may be a risk, this lower pace of margin expansion is very much forgivable if you're a long term investor. I believe the company's commitment to AI is in fact a bullish sign as it shows that management is focused on the future rather than short-termism.

AI Push Is Clear

If Workday's enterprise solutions were truly getting disrupted, adoption would drop sharply. Instead, there are signs that businesses continue to choose Workday. In early to mid March, a press release showed that Fairview Health Services had selected Workday "to modernize HR, finance, and supply chain on One AIPowered Platform." With Fairview being a very large organization with 34,000 employees, this sends a signal that Workday remains highly relevant today.

In addition to continued adoption, innovation is also occurring at Workday. In mid to late March, the company introduced Sana. This is actually a portfolio of AI solutions. You can read more about this in their press release but the launch of Sana for Workday, Sana Self-Service Agent, and Sana Enterprise clearly show that they are on top of their AI game. While investors fear AI disruption, the company itself seems to be going all-in with regards to conversational AI, agentic AI, and enterprise AI.

Valuation At Depressed Levels

As you can see in the chart above, Workday's forward P/E ratio really hasn't had time to catch a break recently. The multiple is now just a hair above multiyear lows and at the current reading of 12.45, I would say that Workday's risk/reward is heavily in the bulls' favor.

Today, it was shown that Workday's fundamentals have been largely resilient. Growth actually accelerated from Q3 and margin also expanded. Moving forward, guidance isn't perfect by any means. However, the estimates also clearly show that an apocalyptic event isn't coming anytime soon. The main risk moving forward is slower margin expansion. As demonstrated by the consensus EPS growth estimates for FY2027, bottom line growth may be weaker than what we have seen and so that could be a risk to near term stock performance. However, for long term investors, I view the AI investments as crucial in order to secure the future of the business. I personally wouldn't mind some short term pain for long term gain.

At current levels, the forward P/E seems to imply that the market thinks the end may be coming for Workday. I believe this conclusion would be laughable. Looking forward, growth is expected to continue being relatively resilient and the AI push discussed above shows that they are very much relevant in this day and age of AI.

Conclusion

In my view, Workday stock has been unjustifiably punished in the past few months. With declines in excess of 40%, I believe the setup is now very favorable for long term investors. This is a classic example of being greedy when others are fearful. SaaSpocalypse fears were shown to be irrational in today's analysis and the real risk is just some slower margin expansion over the near term due to AI investments. The fundamentals and outlook in no rational way justify a valuation that's this severely depressed. As a result, I'm initiating Workday at a buy rating.

data.title: Lumen: Look At The Balance Sheet To Get A Feel For Future Growth
data.description:
If I had to pick one stock that I have failed to crack, my first pick would be Lumen Technologies, Inc. (LUMN). Almost 7 years ago, I made a small profit with LUMN, but since then, the going has been difficult. I was bearish on Lumen when I should have been bullish and vice versa.

Last October, after a couple of years sitting on the sidelines, I turned bullish on the prospects for Lumen as I thought the company was on the right track to grow adjusted EBITDA in 2026. Since then, the stock has lost just over 1% of its value. Last January, I made a case for why Lumen deserves to trade at premium valuation multiples compared to legacy telcos. My argument was that Lumen was no longer a legacy telco business but a tech business. LUMN has lost ~15% since then.

Now that Lumen has reported Q4 2025 earnings and set a baseline for 2026 expectations, I wanted to revisit my thesis. Rather than looking at all aspects of my thesis, I thought it best to look at the timeline for PCF contract revenue conversion cycles to get a better understanding of when Lumen will finally get a revenue boost from the secured PCF contracts.

Understanding When PCF Contracts Hit Revenue

Lumen is solving real-world connectivity problems for hyperscalers who are aggressively investing billions of dollars in AI infrastructure. This is where PCF contracts are used. Internet bandwidth through traditional networks has been proven insufficient to handle the data loads required to train LLM models. A PCF contract allows a customer to get access to a private, custom fiber network with a very high capacity and low latency. Some of Lumen's major PCF customers include tech giants such as Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), Meta Platforms, Inc. (META), Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), Alphabet Inc. (GOOG), and Anthropic (the company behind the widely popular Claude LLM).

As highlighted by the management in the Q4 earnings release, Lumen has so far signed PCF contracts worth $13 billion.

Exhibit 1: Lumen's 2025 financial highlights

Despite stellar growth in PCF contracts, Lumen has not seen a material impact from them on its revenue growth. This is because of how these contracts are packaged. These are often long-term contracts where Lumen has agreed to deploy infrastructure over several years. What this means is that Lumen does not get to report the total contract value as revenue in the current period. Going by the management guidance, we should expect these contracts to contribute at least $500 million to revenue annually before 2030. This could happen as soon as 2028 as well. For at least another 2 years, we are unlikely to see a meaningful impact on Lumen's revenue growth resulting from the signed PCF deals.

Although that may be discouraging, the good news is that Lumen's balance sheet is mostly immune to the capital expenditure requirements arising from the expected buildout of these networks. This is because Lumen collects upfront cash payments from customers who sign PCF contracts. This characteristic of PCF contracts has helped Lumen a great deal to keep its liquidity profile stable in the short run.

A quick look at Lumen's operating cash flow movements and deferred revenue confirms how PCF contracts are currently accounted for. The company's total deferred revenue has ballooned from $2.68 billion in Q1 2024 to $7.41 billion in Q4 2025. Deferred revenue has started accelerating from the fourth quarter of 2024, when Lumen went all-in on PCF contracts. A breakdown of this deferred revenue reveals that Lumen investors are in for a long wait. According to company filings, $6.4 billion of this deferred revenue is classified as long-term deferred revenue, meaning that only $1 billion in deferred revenue is expected to be realized in the next 12 months.

There are clear signs that Lumen has already started the buildout for these PCF contracts. CapEx grew from around $1 billion in Q3 2025 to $1.64 billion in Q4, coinciding with the start of Lumen's aggressive investments in PCF buildouts.

Exhibit 2: Capital expenditures

The absolute CapEx run rate will decline in 2026 because of the recently completed AT&T deal. The resulting CapEx will almost entirely focus on new growth avenues, primarily building the necessary infrastructure to move PCF contract revenue from the balance sheet to the income statement. For now, Lumen is dealing with a lot of challenges while paving the way for an influx of new, high-quality revenue starting in a few years. Lumen's large enterprise segment revenue only increased by $5 million sequentially in Q4, in complete contrast to how PCF contract backlog has grown. Lumen is clearly suffering from a timing issue here. Interestingly, I believe the market is still valuing Lumen based on its continued YoY revenue declines, when in reality, the company has built a strong foundation to reverse the course in the future.

The Current Valuation Fails To Accurately Account For The PCF Backlog

Lumen has seen its revenue erode in the past few years. Profitability has been a distant reality. And the market is valuing Lumen as a legacy telecom business that is about to die. This premise is wrong, in my opinion.

PCF revenues are likely to enjoy much higher EBITDA margins than the dying, legacy business of Lumen. PCF customers are injecting upfront cash to fund the CapEx required for buildouts, so when Lumen reports revenue, margins will be high. In addition to this, the PCF business is likely to have structurally lower maintenance costs compared to the legacy lease business. I don't believe Mr. Market has fully grasped the fact that deferred revenue, which is currently reported as a liability, represents a multi-billion-dollar high-margin revenue stream.

Today, Lumen is valued at a forward EV/EBITDA multiple of 7.9x.

Going by the management guidance, if Lumen were to generate $550 million in ARR from PCF contracts starting in 2028, we are looking at new recurring EBITDA of around $358 million (EBITDA margin of 65% on new PCF revenue).

Here's where the multiple expansion will happen.

Legacy telcos generally trade for around 6x EV/EBITDA. However, digital infrastructure solutions providers typically trade at much higher multiples that sometimes stretch as high as 15x EV/EBITDA. Given Lumen's strong moat within the PCF sector, I believe applying an EV/EBITDA multiple of 12x makes sense. Going by this, the PCF business will be valued at around $4.3 billion.

In simpler terms, I believe the PCF segment itself is worth over $4 billion. Lumen's equity is currently valued at $7.2 billion in the market. I expect the blended EV/EBITDA multiple to expand meaningfully through 2030 as the contribution from the PCF business continues to grow.

Risks

Lumen is still in a business transformation phase. A lot can still go wrong. I am keeping an eye on how the company handles aggressive investments while keeping its balance sheet health in check. Failure to do this will almost certainly lead to a market rout as investors, including myself, still have flashbacks of Lumen's struggles.

Then there is the continued drag on profitability and growth stemming from the dying legacy business. Since this business is eroding much faster than the new PCF and AI businesses, the short-run financial performance is going to look ugly. Amid macro and geopolitical tensions, this could lead to a prolonged decline in the stock price before an eventual recovery. For this reason, I believe LUMN is only suitable for investors with a strong stomach.

Investors cannot and should not forget the debt burden too. Although the balance sheet position is much better than it was a few years ago, post 2027, debt maturities will accelerate.

Takeaway

Lumen is attractively valued. Growth may seem lackluster for now, but a good look at the balance sheet suggests Lumen is positioning itself to benefit from a revenue windfall from PCF contracts in a few years. This is not accurately baked into Lumen's market value today, in my opinion. Although a few risks need to be closely monitored, I believe Lumen is very attractive today.

data.title: Novo Nordisk: Competing In A Duopoly
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Novo Nordisk A/S (NVO) competes almost exclusively against Eli Lilly and Company (LLY) in a fast growing GLP-1 market. Although the falling stock price may make the company cheap, competition and high reliance on patents raise questions regarding the sustainability of future growth. Eli Lilly seems to currently have a slight advantage with tirzepatide, justifying the falling stock price of Novo Nordisk. Nevertheless, Europe and Asia remain attractive markets for Novo Nordisk to capitalize on.

Business Overview

Novo Nordisk is a Danish pharmaceutical company and with a market cap of ~150 billion USD amongst the biggest companies in all of Europe. Their products can be divided into four segments. The most important segment, measured by revenue, is diabetes, accounting for 67% of total revenue. Key products are Ozempic and Rybelsus, accounting for 61% and 11% of Diabetes revenue respectively. The second largest segment is obesity at 26.5% of Total revenue, which mainly consists of Wegovy. Ozempic, Rybelsus and Wegovy all consist of semaglutide, a peptide that imitates glucagon. For diabetics, glucagon is the hormone which transfers sugar from the liver into the blood. Obese people make use of semaglutide since glucagon helps lower the appetite and therefore stops cravings. Side effects of this peptide are nausea, vomiting and diarrhea. Ozempic is administered via injection, while Rybelsus has the same function, just as a oral medication. Wegovy can be accessed via injection or orally. Together, these three medications combined account for 73.7% of total revenue and therefore form the core of Novo Nordisk's business. Earlier this year, Wegovy as a 7.2 mg dose was approved, improving weight loss results to 20.7% in 72 weeks, compared to 16.6% achieved by the original 2.4 mg dose. A small friction of revenue (6%) is generated by the rare disease segment, mainly treating blood disorders. Since I do not believe Novo Nordisk's future depends on this segment, it will not be further discussed.

Geographically, 56% of revenue comes from the US, while the other 44% is split between EUCAN, Emerging markets and Asia, at 21%, 10% and 13%, respectively.

Competing Against Eli Lilly

Since the launch of Ozempic in 2017, Novo Nordisk's entire story relied on GLP-1s. From a stagnating business in 2017 and 2018, growth has been accelerating quickly until 2025, while keeping net income margins stable at around 34%.

Although they own around 39% market share in the US, their closest competitor, Eli Lilly, is taking most of the market growth and even some share of Novo Nordisk. Eli Lilly's tirzepatide directly competes against semaglutide and outperforms it significantly. A 72 weeks trial comparing the two shows that tirzepatide reduced weight by 7% more than semaglutide 2.4 mg. This is similar to the results of Wegovy 7.2 mg, although tirzepatide is already on the market through its diabetes version, Mounjaro, and its obesity version called Zepbound. The reason for this advantage is due to tirzepatide using GLP-1 combined with GIP molecules, compared to semaglutide only using GLP-1. GIP increases the feeling of fullness, but only in combination with GLP-1s and cannot be used on its own. In 2025, this stopped Novo to grow by more than 6%. Eli Lilly's more effective products led to Novo Nordisk guiding negative sales growth of -5% to -13%, while Eli Lilly management anticipates 25% growth in 2026. The decrease in sales is worsened by the US government forcing both companies to significantly lower the price of their medications. In response to the Wegovy pill for oral intake released earlier this year, Eli Lilly will release Orfoglipron in Q2 this year. After 72 weeks, Orfoglipron achieved 11.2% weight loss, noticeably losing to the 16.6% weight loss achieved by the Wegovy pill in just 68 weeks. Overall, Eli Lilly's main advantage lies in its tirzepatide for injections. Pill wise, the Wegovy pill is more effective than Orfoglipron.

Growth Drivers

However, history is not an indicator of the future. I believe there are two main reasons, that drive the future success of Novo Nordisk.

1. GLP-1 market growth

According to JPMorgan, 30 million people in the US will use GLP-1s in 2030. Based of an initial 10 million customers in 2025, this suggests a CAGR of around 25% in the next five years. Eli Lilly seems to be clearly dominating the market, owning 60% market share and growing, while Novo Nordisk's market share has been decreasing massively.

However, there are markets outside the US. Another study shows that the global market will also grow by 25% until 2029, with the US approximately accounting for 53% of the GLP-1 market. This suggests that the other half of growth lies in International Operations. To grow at 25% globally, each region has to grow at the same rate. In 2025, Novo Nordisk generated the majority of its international growth from Europe and Asia. In Europe, Novo Nordisk achieved 14.9% growth, as the leading GLP-1 medication is considered to be Wegovy. However, Eli Lilly is growing even quicker at around 66%, with Zepbound being the fastest growing GLP-1 medication in Europe. Asia shows a more even picture. Novo Nordisk grew by 18.8%, compared to Eli Lilly's 16.6%. Therefore, Novo Nordisk seems to lose market share in Europe while slightly winning shares in Asia. Hence, it is vital for Novo Nordisk to win back market share in Europe and the US to grow alongside with the market, as the Asian market seems to be evenly split between the two.

2. Medicine coming from the current pipeline

In pharmacy, innovations are demanding a lot of time. From a new drug discovery up to the market launch, 13 years of testing are required for approval. Thus, the pipeline of upcoming products in the next years is set. At the end of 2025, Novo Nordisk signed for approval of CagriSema, a medication combining the effects of semaglutide and cagrilintide. Cagrilintide mimics the feeling of fullness rather than lower the appetite. CagriSema achieved 23% weight loss in 84 weeks, which once again is a significant improvement from Wegovy 7.4mg. The US approval for CagriSema is expected around the end of 2026, with a possible launch in 2027. In about three years from now, a new drug named zenagamtide will have completed testing trials and be ready to launch. Zenagamtide combines semaglutide and amylin in just one molecule. While CagriSema requires separate storage of semaglutide and cagrilintide in a pen, one molecule in zenagamtide means that there is need for just one storage in a pen, making the dosing much more cost effective. So far, trial results have been astonishing. After just 36 weeks, participants lost an average of 24.3% of their initial weight. This means that the drug takes just half the time compared to Wegovy to provide almost 10% better results. In response to Novo Nordisk's zenagamtide, Eli Lilly is testing retatrutide, which combines GLP-1s, GIP and Glucagon for increased effectiveness. After 48 weeks, retatrutide achieved 24.2%, similar zenagamtide, but required 12 more weeks. Retatrutide is set to launch at the beginning of 2028, while zenagamtide will launch at the beginning of 2029, if FDA approves both medications.

I believe all upcoming products do not accelerate market growth but target to grow or remain current market share levels. At the time of the JPMorgan report, oral Wegovy had already been released and was therefore taken into account in growth estimates. I do not think that retatrutide and zenagamtide, with more effective, results are a convincing argument for people that are generally not comfortable with GLP-1s. Rather, I believe that customers who are on less effective medications, such as Wegovy 2.4 mg, will switch to zenagamtide, creating a cannibalization effect.

The pipeline can be influenced by acquisitions of smaller companies that are already into testing their medications. As an example, during Q4 of 2025, Novo Nordisk lost a bidding war to Pfizer Inc. (PFE) for Metsera. Metsera is conducting trials on amycretin injections, that could require a once-a-month injection rather than a weekly one. It is hard to anticipate acquisitions for multiple years into the future, which is why I will not further discuss the potential impact on Novo Nordisk's business.

Risks

The biggest risk for sustainable future growth for Novo Nordisk lies in its patents. Ozempic and Wegovy will no longer be a strong growth driver for Novo Nordisk in 2031/2032, when other companies are allowed to flood the market with generics, and when brand loyalty may face significant pressure. More effective ingredients, like zenagamtide and retatrutide, require new patents, which should prevent Novo Nordisk and Eli Lilly from being substituted by generics. Nevertheless, I do not believe the patents of more effective ingredients to be as game-changing as for example the Wegovy patent, since customers will have a slightly less effective, but still a feasible alternative in generic semaglutide.

Further risks come with regulation. For example, in 2025, the US government forced Novo Nordisk to significantly lower the prices of Wegovy and Ozempic by $400-$600. This impacts revenue and margins massively and could make future medications unprofitable and unattractive compared to the associated R&D costs. While this is not foreseeable, it could still have very meaningful impact on Novo Nordisk's business model.

Valuation

I use a DCF model to determine Novo Nordisks intrinsic value. Since I disagree with using beta to calculate the cost of equity, I used a 15% discount rate as a hurdle rate. I use net income instead of free cash flow because standard FCF metrics exclude stock-based compensation. Using net income treats SBC as a real expense, accounting for shareholder dilution. For my valuation, I considered how the 25% market growth would split each year between Novo Nordisk and Eli Lilly. In 2031 and 2032, I assumed a market growth rate of 20% instead of 25% as the market gets larger over time and therefore harder to grow.

In 2026, I assumed that lower US prices combined with low awareness of the Wegovy pill would decrease revenue to the more optimistic end of management guidance. I think that higher Wegovy pill awareness will lead to a relatively even split of growth in 2027 between Novo Nordisk and Eli Lilly since Mounjaro and Zepbound are still more effective injections. Margin should decrease slightly, as Wegovy pills are not as profitable as injections. As retatrutide launches in 2028 with low awareness, I believe Novo Nordisk will still grow by high single digits to low double digits on further decreasing margins. The launch of zenagamtide will lift growth and margin again in 2029 before the growth is split equally in 2030 and 2031. Based on these assumptions, I arrive at a fair share price value of $37, which is slightly above current market prices of $36. The exchange rate used is 0.16 DKK/USD.

Conclusion

Novo Nordisk has a competitive pipeline of upcoming products in the future, defending from Eli Lilly to keep taking market share and acquiring more customers in Asia. Nevertheless, comparing current injection offerings, like Wegovy and Zepbound, definitely justify Novo Nordisks stock price. Although the Wegovy pill seems to be more effective than Orfoglipron, available market growth is limited and I believe Novo Nordisk taking market share from Eli Lilly is unlikely. My valuation model indicates a fair stock price of $37 to receive shareholder returns of 15% annually. Therefore, I rate Novo Nordisk as 'Hold'

data.title: Delek US Holdings: Iran War Benefits Justify The Rally (Downgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Delek US Holdings (DK) have been an extraordinary performer over the past year, nearly tripling in value. The company has been a primary beneficiary of an easier regulatory environment for smaller refineries, as the EPA granted a backlog of exemptions (“SRE”) from renewable fuel standard requirements in 2025. More recently, the Iran War has caused crack spreads to surge, enhancing refiner profitability. I last covered shares in January, rating DK a “Strong Buy,” and this call has worked out better than hoped, with shares up 54% in just three months. With such strong performance and a new macro environment, now is a good time to revisit Delek.

Fuel disruptions lead to a surge in crack spreads

Refiners make money not on the absolute price of gasoline or diesel but on the spread between product prices and crude prices, known as the crack spread. This measures how much refiners make for every barrel of crude oil. As you can see below, the spread has blown out since the outbreak of the Iran War, given impaired flows through the Strait of Hormuz. With disruptions of 10-12 million barrels a day, I expect the cumulative shortfall from this crisis to be at least 500 million barrels, a shock of extraordinary proportions.

Crack spreads are now sitting over $40, having even gotten past $60 as the physical market scrambles for product amid fears of shortages, especially in products like jet fuel and diesel. The United States is better positioned than Europe or much of Asia, given we are a net exporter of fuel, making physical shortages unlikely in my view. However, the pull from overseas will likely lift demand for U.S. exports and is contributing to higher spreads. This has created a windfall environment for refineries like Delek, greatly boosting their cash flow.

This one-time benefit justifies the recent rally

As a reminder, I view the ~$25 crack spread level as a long-term norm. When I value refining stocks, I tend to use this level to get an estimate of fair value, recognizing that in the short run, spreads can be very volatile. I tend to view these as more one-time in nature, and in this case, Delek and the industry are enjoying a tremendous windfall. Consider that in 2025, Delek’s average throughput was about 303,000bpd. At an extra ~$15 crack spread ($40 vs. $25), the company makes about $3.3 million of extra free cash flow every day. That is over ~$0.05 of EPS per day. This crisis has already gone on for 34 days, providing a $1.70 windfall.

This likely understates the true impact, as spreads have been averaging over $40, though given its footprint is in the mid-continent, Delek likely has enjoyed lower spreads than Gulf refiners closer to the export market. In theory, if this crisis became the new normal, DK would have another ~$18 of earnings power. Of course, I do not think we can sustain $40+ crack spreads forever. At some point, the flow through the Strait of Hormuz will open, other production will ramp up, or high prices will destroy demand to create a new equilibrium.

Before the invasion, DK shares were at about $37, so we have seen an ~$8 rally. With crack spreads having spent a month between $40 and $60, DK has likely enjoyed a ~$2-$3 benefit already. Even if the flow through the Strait reopened as normal tomorrow, it will take time to normalize production, as Mideast states have been forced to curtail operations. I believe it will take 3-4 weeks after a true reopening of the Strait to get back to normal. With President Trump signaling 2-3 more weeks of fighting, an immediate reopening to normal levels is unlikely in my view.

As such, I expect this current crack spread environment to persist for at least 5 more weeks, meaning DK should ultimately see a benefit during the Strait closure of at least $5, largely explaining the rally over the past month. I also suspect even after reopening, markets will be uneasy and spreads wider for a bit. Beyond this one-time benefit, shares are essentially up ~$3. For perspective, if crack spreads were to run at ~$26 vs. $25 over the long term, given a bit more geopolitical premiums, DK would have ~$1.20 of added earnings power, more than justifying ~$3 of share price gains.

DK fundamentals have also improved thanks to regulatory relief

To a large extent, the rally in Delek over the past month reflects one-time benefits from the war, and I do not see markets getting ahead of themselves and extrapolating an excessively bullish scenario. Indeed, a much longer-term disruption of 6+ months would likely lead to a substantially larger windfall than shares are currently reflecting. Additionally, it is important to emphasize that Delek was performing well even before the breakout of this conflict.

The company swung to a $2.31 profit in Q4, far ahead of consensus. This included a ~$150 million benefit from the EPA’s SRE decision. Absent this, EPS was $0.44, still better than expected for a modest loss. Alongside results, the company upped the target of its cost-saving program to $200 million and restructured its inventory agreement with Citi (C), which should boost free cash flow another $40 million. In total, regulatory relief was $356 million in 2025. With its refineries smaller than the 75kb/d capacity cap, Delek is the refiner best positioned from the EPA’s grant of exemptions.

The company has a stellar balance sheet with $615 million of cash and just $889 million of debt, excluding Delek Logistics (DKL). As a reminder, DK owns nearly 34 million shares of DKL, worth about $1.7 billion, or ~$27 per DK share. I like to value DK on a stand-alone basis and then add back the equity value of its DKL stake. With a solid balance sheet and significant free cash flow benefit from wider spreads, I expect DK to buy back at least $125 million of shares this year, and I view its 2.3% dividend yield as highly secure.

DK still has sufficient upside to buy

I see DK now having about $120 million of long-term free cash flow (at a normalized $25 crack spread), or about $2/share. Given the cyclicality of refining, I would value DK at 10x free cash flow or with a stand-alone value of $20. Adding in its DKL stake, shares are worth $48. However, the company is also likely to see a windfall this year from the Iran war of at least $5.

That makes fair value at least $53, which assumes a fully reopened Strait by June 1. If it goes on for longer, fair value would move higher (at about $1.70/month). While shares have surged, I see another ~20% total return potential. As such, it is still not too late to own DK. After a 50% run, I do not view shares as a “Strong Buy,” but I still see them as a “Buy” at current levels. I would continue to add DK and expect shares to rally past $50.

data.title: FLTW: An AI Infra Bet Dressed Up As An EM ETF
data.description:
Franklin FTSE Taiwan ETF (FLTW) on the surface looks like a nice way to get broad exposure to a dynamic emerging market. However, its extreme concentration in AI infrastructure with 21.7% in Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM) alone means it’s heavy on chips, packaging, servers, and power. It’s essentially a semiconductor and artificial intelligence infrastructure bet padded by some financials, which make up 15% as a sector. Investors should therefore know what they’re getting into before committing. Due to an already impressive rally and the cyclical nature of the product, I rate FLTW a Hold.

Holdings Concentration

Right off the bat we can see that Tech makes up more than 72% of holdings, followed by financials at 15%. Names like MediaTek, Hon Hai Precision Industry Co., Ltd. (HNHAF) (better known as Foxconn), Delta Electronics (DLEGF), and ASE Technology Holdings Co., Ltd. (ASX) are also AI-adjacent, and mostly operate in the hardware supply chain. These companies account for some 21% of holdings which together with Taiwan Semiconductor makes 44.4%. You’re not getting QQQ-like exposure to software and platforms, but are in the tech sleeve purely banking on infrastructure.

FLTW tracks the FTSE Taiwan Capped Index, which is market-cap weighted and limits how big the concentration of any one stock or sector can get. However, due to the nature of the market cap methodology and index rules, the fund is naturally geared towards the tech sector. It rebalances every quarter, which means that the top holdings retain their dominance and AI infrastructure is the main focus. Although financials provide some broader exposure and dividends, and small index adjustments can change weights slightly (1-3%), investors must keep in mind that the composition of the ETF is likely to stay structurally geared towards the top holdings.

Taiwan Semiconductor is not only the largest holding, but the earnings engine that drives a significant portion of the portfolio. The companies around it in fact amplify Taiwan Semiconductor’s earnings during the cycle, as money moves from demand for photolithography machines, to chip designers, to its orders, to Taiwanese supply chain revenue. As such, we should take a closer look at Taiwan Semiconductor’s earnings profile.

TSMC Earnings

Taiwan Semiconductor is a global semiconductor powerhouse with a very broad moat. However, it is not immune to the cyclical nature of the semiconductor industry. The stock is up 103.5% over the last year and is now trading at 21.8x forward earnings. This isn’t cheap, but the growth seems to back it up.

In Jan-Feb 2026, revenue increased by 30% year-over-year, but the 20.8% sequential decline in February shows how lumpy and volatile earnings can be. Also, making this money costs money. Capex of $45 billion for fab construction, capacity installations and packaging upgrades is not cheap, and creates both barriers to entry and risk.

Due to the ETF’s concentration in Taiwan Semiconductor and related companies, the fund’s performance is directly tied to the semiconductor industry. Taiwan Semiconductor's volatility is the ETF’s volatility, and although the fund is diversified in name, it’s not so diversified in actual performance. High CapEx amplifies upside during demand spikes, and downside during slowdowns. Taiwan Semiconductor’s valuation leaves little room for error at the moment, and as such, the ETF inherits Taiwan Semiconductor’s risk profile as well as its growth.

Performance Is Tied To Semiconductor Cycle

The theory is already visible in performance. FLTW is up 57.5% over the last year versus a 23% increase for QQQ. Interestingly, this reflects investor demand for upstream producers of chips, manufacturing, and infrastructure, rather than downstream companies that focus on software, monetization and platforms. Essentially, the market thinks we’re in a build-out heavy phase rather than a monetization-heavy phase of the cycle.

At a forward P/E of 21.8x, the market is already pricing in growth. Indeed, EPS is expected to grow by 36.5% in 2026 and 23.5% in 2027. This is strong, but decelerating. Moreover, with 8 upside revisions, expectations already reflect strong growth, so further upside requires additional positive developments as the bar for earnings surprises becomes harder to clear.

Risks and Catalysts

The primary risk to the Hold thesis is that the semiconductor industry enters a sustained downcycle, reducing demand for chips. For example, if spending from companies like NVDIA Corporation (NVDA) slows down, this will weaken the current build-out phase and hit the income statement of the fund’s top holdings directly.

Another risk is tied to valuation. At 21.8x forward earnings, strong growth is already reflected in the valuation and earnings estimates, and missing these fairly high bars would disappoint and lead to lower multiples. Geopolitical risks related to Chinese aggression remain, and while fairly low probability at the moment, would disrupt the entire Taiwanese economy.

On the catalyst side, sustained or accelerating demand for AI-related hardware would support revenue growth, extending the current upcycle and paving the way for earnings beats and positive revisions. If supply constraints for advanced chips tighten, the industry could start to see higher margins which would translate to higher returns down the line.

Conclusion

Franklin FTSE Taiwan ETF does not offer broad exposure to EM, but is rather a concentrated bet on the semiconductor industry and AI infrastructure in particular. With more than 70% in tech and 21.7% in Taiwan Semiconductor in particular, the stock and the industry become the driver of both risks and returns. Although the industry is seeing strong growth and continued earnings strength, expectations are already high and execution becomes even more important. Cyclicality, valuation, and geopolitical risk all cap the risk/reward profile at the moment. Overall, the ETF offers a balanced setup for investors bullish on semiconductors and AI hardware.

data.title: UiPath: Buying Agentic AI At A 40% Discount
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Analyzing UiPath (PATH) and the root causes of its 30% decline in market value in 2026, we must not confuse volatility with the risk of a loss in the company’s fundamental value. The agency AI solutions sector has undergone a synchronized correction since my first recommendation, behind which lies a massive transformation of PATH. In 2026, agency AI resembles the "Wild West" era, where both IT giants like Microsoft (MSFT) and agile startups offering automation with direct code are entering the fray. In this context, PATH is pursuing a strategy of transitioning from a niche RPA player to a comprehensive AI solutions platform that enables the automation of organizations’ business processes.

In parallel with high market volatility, the quarterly report from PATH exceeded expectations, starting with revenue and net profit growth and ending with updated forecasts for the coming calendar year. Analysts estimate the RPA market at $28.3 billion, but the company's transition to the agent AI segment allows it to scale its business by dividing the total market pie of $116 billion. This article identifies factors demonstrating PATH’s investment appeal, starting with a huge 40% discount to the median multiples of direct competitors, through to new partnerships and growth in orders for AI-based products. Therefore, despite pressure from new competitors and increased market volatility, I am reaffirming my "Buy" rating.

Previous Theses

The thesis underlying my previous research on UiPath has been the investment attractiveness of UiPath as an asset with strategic potential stemming from its decision to transition to a platform-based business model featuring AI-powered solutions. The first positive effects for the company, though, will not be seen until Q1 2026 at the earliest, and so the stock is still highly sensitive to market volatility. A result, PATH's market value has declined by 22% from the time of the first recommendation, which is partly due to the overall market conditions, with a decline in investor demand for risky assets. Another important factor in this price decline is the entry of new competitors into the market for agent-based AI solutions, including cloud giants and promising startups. The fact that the market for agent-based AI solutions is under pressure because of the market situation is confirmed by the stock performance since the beginning of 2026 for the main companies that are direct competitors to PATH. Those assets show a similar stock price decline of around 30%.

UiPath’s AI Products and Platform Business Model

The competitive advantage of PATH begins with a strategic choice to develop and promote an intelligent platform based on RPA products, encompassing a wide range of agent-based AI services. This has resulted in the company’s transition from a traditional player in the RPA segment to a full-fledged competitor in the agent-based AI market. These developments could enable PATH to scale significantly in the future, since the RPA market is projected to reach $28.3 billion by the end of 2026, whereas the agent-based AI market, growing at an average annual rate of 19%, in 2026 will reach $116.6 billion, growing to $558 billion by 2035. Its products are targeted at the B2B segment, helping client organizations optimize business processes of any complexity by implementing agent-based AI.

Due to this transformation, PATH’s business model has shifted from a tool-based model to a platform-based one, allowing it to overcome the limitation of a restricted service offering. This platform-based business model enables the company to expand its services and make them more comprehensive, automatically increasing the average contract value per client. Additionally, the expansion of the product portfolio has included the integration of new AI technologies, serving as the foundation of the commercial offering for customers interested in developing their management infrastructure.

In transitioning to agent-based AI, PATH has formed strategic partnerships with the following IT giants.

PATH's Competitive Environment

Competition in PATH's market is intensifying, with players in the RPA segment (Automation Anywhere and SS&C Blue Prism) now joined by companies offering similar services in the agent-based AI solutions segment. As I noted earlier, some of the most aggressive new competitors include Pegasystems (PEGA), Appian (APPN), and Kofax. The first company has similar AI solutions integrated into RPA; the second is focused on end-to-end process automation using its proprietary AI platform; the third provides a unified automation platform using a combination of AI solutions and RPA.

But the competition doesn’t end there, as the fast-growing agency AI solutions market is giving rise to new products offered by various software companies. Despite its partnership with PATH, Microsoft is the most formidable competitor, since it offers similar services to companies that are deeply integrated into the Windows ecosystem. The advantage of MSFT is the almost zero cost of entry for M365/Azure users and deep integration with Copilot and Azure AI. As a result, MSFT offers a simpler licensing model that allows it to poach customers who previously used PATH’s services.

The other key competitor vying for market share in the business process automation sector is Automation Anywhere. This company focuses on Agent-based Process Automation through its IQ Bot solution, enabling effective handling of unstructured data. Looking ahead to 2026, the company is banking on openness to third-party AI agents, presenting itself as a cloud-oriented platform. One more competitor is ServiceNow (NOW), going through a similar business model transformation to transition to a full-fledged platform. Because there are a bunch of customers who already use this company’s services, it’s pretty easy for them to switch to the new products.

Additionally, the field of agency AI solutions is evolving rapidly, leading to the launch of new platforms. The first quarter of 2026, in particular, was marked by the launch of products from startups such as CrewAI, Dify, and Lyzard. Often, these solutions involve direct interaction between their AI agents via APIs and code, presenting a long-term risk to PATH. This is because such platforms can learn to solve tasks without relying on a visual interface. Consequently, UiPath, who decided to shift its competitive advantage toward a unified control center for all AI agents, has become a target of greater scrutiny in the market. Should PATH lose out to the competition, it will rapidly lose market share to players offering cheaper solutions, such as those offered via cloud services.

Key Highlights of the Quarterly Report for Q4 FY2026

The last quarterly report from PATH was positive, since the actual results exceeded analysts’ consensus estimates. EPS was $0.30, up $0.05 from the forecast, with revenue coming in $16.3 million higher ($481.11 million, or +13.5% compared to the same period a year earlier). While it was previously expected that the main effects of PATH’s transformation would be felt starting in Q1 2026, the company’s AI-based products have already increased ARR by $200 million. The number of customers with deals exceeding $100,000 increased by 26%, while 16 of the 20 largest deals closed in Q4 2025 included products based on agent-based AI solutions.

In addition, it presented an updated forecast for fiscal year 2027, which anticipates revenue in the range of $1.754–1.759 billion, up from the previous forecast of $1.74 billion. Projected revenue for the first quarter has also been raised in the forecasts from $393.36 million to $395–$400 million. Furthermore, the ARR as of April 30, 2026, and January 31, 2027, was expected to be in the ranges of $1.894–$1.899 billion and $2.051–$2.056 billion, accordingly.

PATH Valuation

In order to assess PATH’s investment appeal, forward values of key multiples (EV/Sales and EV/EBITDA) should be analyzed in comparison with other competitors MSFT, PEGA, APPN, and NOW.

Median values for the EV/Sales and EV/EBITDA multiples are 4.60x and 14.55x, respectively. That gives corresponding discounts of 48.04% and 32.51%. Overall, we get a median discount of 40.27%. These results indicate that PATH shares are significantly undervalued based on key multiples, which supports the high assessment of the company’s investment appeal.

Risks for PATH

As of now, I think that, apart from the competitive environment, two other risks could impact the outlook for PATH’s stock.

The first is the risk associated with the successful integration of new AI products into PATH’s business strategy, because now the company’s financial results depend to a greater extent on the effectiveness of its strategic partnerships. Most of the effects of the transition to the new business model will not be felt until at least Q1 2026, meaning that the next earnings report may already provide initial answers to questions about how effective the new products in the agency AI space are.

Second, the first risk is exacerbated by macroeconomic conditions, the situation of which may deteriorate due to the current global instability. The geopolitical situation in the Middle East is affecting energy prices, which increases the likelihood of rising inflation. While inflation expectations aren’t showing aggressive growth, it’s possible the Fed might take preemptive action by ending monetary easing and shifting to a tightening phase. Such decisions would lead to continued selling by market participants, impacting the market value of all stock market assets, PATH included.

Conclusion

Despite the emergence of new risks and threats, I maintain my previous "Buy" rating. The company is at a turning point as it develops new software integrated with AI technologies. These new products, the foundation of the platform-based business model, have been actively distributed, which allows most of the major contracts to be secured through their sale. Ultimately, the management of PATH is targeting an increase in ARR to $2 billion, a figure that will reflect the robustness of the platform and the expansion of the customer portfolio. And the target operating margin has been adjusted from 20% to 30%, reflecting growing confidence in the efficient use of operating resources. Moreover, this technical correction in the stock price presents a more attractive opportunity to buy PATH at a lower price. That increases the potential for further growth in the company’s market capitalization, because there is a huge 40% discount relative to direct competitors based on EV/Sales and EV/EBITDA multiples.

data.title: UiPath: With Meaningful ARR Growth, This Company Has True AI Tailwinds
data.description:
Is AI a net headwind or a tailwind? This seems to be the core question that is dogging the stock market this year, sparking the "SaaSpocalypse" that has wiped billions off the market cap of the software industry across companies both large and small. All in all, I continue to hold the belief that the SaaSpocalypse narrative is overblown, and investors should look to near-term quarterly results for hints whether certain software companies are disruptors or candidates for disruption. After all, customers of these software companies can vote with their feet, and if a certain platform is no longer compelling, customers can churn out, especially in this tough macroeconomy.

UiPath (PATH), which was in the business of workflow and business process automation long before AI became a buzzword, looks like a company enjoying tailwinds. Though its share price has contracted ~30% since the start of the year (largely along with other software companies), the company's fundamentals have continued to shine, with healthy net expansion rates and ARR growth.

I last wrote a "Buy" article on UiPath in December, when the stock was trading at $18 per share. Needless to say, the sharp crash in the stock since then has disappointed me, and my buy call was ill-timed (as I certainly did not predict the SaaSpocalypse at the time). That said, such a sharp correction in any stock merits a fresh look, and especially when I assess the strength of UiPath's latest results against its freshly lowered valuation, I find an incredibly attractive bargain play. I'm reiterating my "Buy" rating here.

Here's the reality that we need to confront with UiPath. Since agentic AI took off and automation became the core focus of CIOs and CFOs, UiPath's product is no longer incredibly unique. But at the same time, companies wishing to deploy automation tools now have two distinct options: DIY and build their own vibe-coded, agentic AI tools, or deploy proven existing tools. The former may be more customizable and cheaper, but the latter delivers enterprise support and faster time to value. UiPath can back up its product with years of experience in automating corporate workflows, a wide ecosystem of third-party integrations (which DIY tools may fail to incorporate), and strong recognition from unbiased industry reviewers (Forrester, one of the leading software industry reviewers, named UiPath a leader in autonomous testing platforms).

And yet the stock market is essentially treating UiPath as if it's a prime candidate for AI disruption, even though the company's double-digit revenue growth, ARR expansion, and net revenue retention rates suggest this is all but true.

In the meantime, as we wait for the stock market to correct upward, we have a chance to buy UiPath at incredibly favorable valuations. At current share prices near $11, UiPath trades at a market cap of $5.85 billion. Netting off the $1.70 billion of cash against zero debt on the company's latest balance sheet (another tremendous reason to like this stock) gives us an enterprise value of $4.14 billion.

For FY27 (the year for UiPath ending in January 2027), the company has guided to revenue of $1.754-$1.759 billion, or 9% y/y growth. It's expecting a $415 million pro forma operating profit, or a 23.6% margin, representing 60bps of y/y expansion.

This positions UiPath at just 2.4x EV/FY27 revenue. For a company that can point to AI tailwinds, is close to executing at the "Rule of 40", and has a buildup in ARR as well as healthy net retention, this is quite a steal.
