We note, of course, that the entire software sector has seen a contraction in multiples, and yet when we stack up UiPath against other ~10% growth software companies, we still find UiPath is an outlier on the low end of the industry's revenue multiples.

We also note that the company is taking advantage of its ironclad balance sheet and low share prices: it recently announced a fresh $500 million buyback plan, worth ~9% of the company's current market cap and fully fundable from current cash reserves. With this refreshed buyback authorization, UiPath can make use of this cheap window to offset dilution:

Stay long here and use UiPath's sharp slide this year as a buying opportunity.

Q4 download

Let's now go through UiPath's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

UiPath's revenue grew 14% y/y to $481.1 million, beating Wall Street's expectations of $464.8 million (+10% y/y) by a meaningful four-point margin.

Importantly, we note, as shown in the chart below, that the company added a substantial amount of net-new ARR in the quarter. Total ARR grew by $71 million sequentially in the quarter, rising 11% y/y to $1.85 billion. We note that guidance for FY27 doesn't imply any deceleration in ARR growth, with year-end ARR expected at $2.05 billion (+11% y/y). The fact that UiPath's ARR continues to exceed annual revenue positions the company well for consistent, reliable top-line growth.

We note as well that dollar-based net retention clocked in at 107%, flat to Q3 and indicating that the average customer is spending 7% y/y more on the UiPath platform. Neither the strong net expansion trends nor healthy ARR adds suggest that UiPath is struggling to win and retain business in the AI era, despite the stock market's pessimistic reading on the company.

In fact, management continues to cite AI as a tailwind, with more and more customers attaching the company's agentic AI solutions to their subscription packages. Per CEO Daniel Dines' remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

The most important story this quarter is the economic shift underway inside our installed base. Customers are not experimenting with AI, they are expanding their operating model on our platform.

AI product ARR, which includes agentic, IDP and Maestro, reached nearly $200 million this quarter, with strong growth fueled by agentic. But the number I keep coming back to is this, the number of customers above $100,000 in ARR who have bought AI products grew 25% year-over-year and they spend nearly 3x as much as those who have not. Additionally, 16 of our top 20 deals this quarter included AI products. All of this is clear evidence that agentic automation is becoming central to our largest customers' roadmaps.

Importantly, this AI growth is layering on top of a core unattended automation business that continues to grow. We are not seeing AI agents replacing deterministic unattended automation in production we are seeing customers extending their processes with AI.

UiPath also maintained healthy profitability. GAAP operating margins more than doubled y/y to 17%, while pro forma operating margins held firm at 31% (rising 6 points y/y on a full-year basis to 23%, with further margin improvements expected in FY27).

Risks and key takeaways

Of course, there are also risks that we need to keep in mind here. UiPath may have been the incumbent in the automation space, but as many software companies release their own agentic AI tools to address more function-specific use cases (such as Salesforce's Agentforce products that automate sales and marketing workflows), UiPath may lose share as a more generalist competitor. We're also wary of the tighter IT budget environment and the tough macro, which may slow down new deal activity: UiPath has issued fairly aggressive guidance calling for ~$200 million in net-new ARR adds this year.

That said, we think these risks are already priced into the stock at just ~2x revenue. Take advantage of this year's slide to buy this attractive stock at a discount.
